title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice on a website:

'We want reviews of science museums, planetariums, or science-themed exhibitions. Have you visited one recently? Write a review describing your experience and saying whether you would recommend it.'

Write your review.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Review: The Natural History Museum — London

The Natural History Museum in London is one of the world's great scientific institutions, and a recent visit confirmed why it continues to attract millions of visitors every year. The building itself is spectacular — a vast Victorian Gothic cathedral filled with the wonders of the natural world.

The dinosaur gallery remains the undisputed highlight. The enormous skeleton of a blue whale suspended from the ceiling of the central hall is breathtaking, and the interactive displays throughout the museum bring science to life in a way that engages visitors of all ages. The Darwin Centre, which houses the museum's scientific research collections, offers a fascinating glimpse behind the scenes of real scientific work.

A particular strength is the museum's commitment to contemporary issues. Exhibits on climate change and biodiversity loss are handled with both scientific rigour and genuine urgency.

Best of all, entry to the permanent collections is free, making it one of the most extraordinary cultural experiences available in the city at no cost.

Absolutely essential. Rating: 5/5

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt:
You have read a newspaper article arguing that science funding should be reduced in favour of arts and humanities programmes. Write a letter to the newspaper editor disagreeing with this view and arguing for the importance of investment in scientific research.

Write your letter.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Editor,

I read with considerable concern the opinion piece published in last Thursday's edition, which argued that government funding for scientific research should be reduced in order to support arts and humanities programmes. While I am a firm believer in the value of the arts, I believe this argument presents a false choice.

Scientific research underpins virtually every aspect of modern life, from the medicines that keep us healthy to the technologies that power our economy. The vaccines developed during recent global health crises, the renewable energy systems addressing climate change, and the agricultural innovations feeding a growing global population are all products of sustained investment in science.

Furthermore, basic scientific research — the kind that may not have immediate commercial application — has historically produced some of humanity's most important discoveries. Cutting such funding for short-term financial reasons would be deeply shortsighted.

The arts and sciences are not rivals. Both deserve support. I urge the government to recognise that investing in science is investing in our collective future.

Yours faithfully,
Dr. Amelia Park

tasks.title: Article
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice in an English-language magazine:

'A new scientific discovery or technological innovation is announced almost every week. Write an article about a recent scientific development that excites or concerns you, and discuss what impact you think it could have on society.'

Write your article.
tasks.modelAnswer:
The Age of AI: Exciting Future or Cause for Concern?

In the past two years, artificial intelligence has moved from the realm of science fiction into everyday reality with startling speed. AI tools can now write essays, generate realistic images, hold conversations, and diagnose diseases. Few technologies in history have advanced so rapidly — and the implications are enormous.

The potential benefits are genuinely exciting. AI is already helping doctors detect cancers earlier, enabling scientists to model complex climate systems, and making education more personalised and accessible. For millions of people worldwide, it could represent access to expertise previously reserved for the wealthy few.

And yet the risks demand serious attention. The potential for AI to eliminate jobs at scale, generate convincing misinformation, and be weaponised for surveillance raises profound ethical questions that society has barely begun to address.

My view is that AI is neither saviour nor villain — it is a tool of extraordinary power. Whether it improves or damages society depends entirely on the values and governance frameworks we build around it.

The question is not whether AI will change the world. It already has. The question is whether we are ready.
